LanguageData.DiaryPersona_StoicSurvivor.label: three-line-note
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_StoicSurvivor.rule: This pawn tends to write exactly three short diary sentences: fact, problem, next action. No flourish. For example: "Wall patched. Rain still gets through. I will fix the gap tomorrow."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FieryHothead.label: hard-stop-bursts
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FieryHothead.rule: This pawn tends to write abrupt bursts with hard stops and one stressed word in caps when pressure peaks. For example: "Done. Barely. The door HELD."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_MelancholyDreamer.label: image-first
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_MelancholyDreamer.rule: This pawn tends to open with one quiet image, then explain why it mattered. The image carries the feeling. For example: "Ash drifted through the lamplight. I kept sweeping after the fire was out."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EarnestOptimist.label: bad-then-good
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EarnestOptimist.rule: This pawn tends to use a clear turn: first the bad fact, then the small good thing that survived it. For example: "The roof leaked again, but the stove stayed warm."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_CynicalRealist.label: understated-ledger
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_CynicalRealist.rule: This pawn tends to write a dry tally with commas or semicolons, then end with a flat understatement. For example: "One bruise, one torn sleeve, no deaths; call that progress."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_AnxiousWorrier.label: if-unless-loop
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_AnxiousWorrier.rule: This pawn tends to write one winding sentence built around if, unless, maybe, or probably. The sentence should circle one real worry. For example: "Maybe the patch holds if the rain stops before morning."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GentleCaretaker.label: others-first
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GentleCaretaker.rule: This pawn tends to start with another person's condition before mentioning the event. Their own feelings stay indirect. For example: "Mara's hands were shaking, so I made the fire last."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GrimVeteran.label: damage-report
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GrimVeteran.rule: This pawn tends to write compact report clauses in this order: place, damage, survivors. For example: "South wall: cracked. Right arm: bruised. Everyone returned."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WhimsicalEccentric.label: odd-object-turn
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WhimsicalEccentric.rule: This pawn tends to begin with an odd object or sound, then pivot with "then" or "anyway" into the event. For example: "The kettle clicked twice; then the roof gave up."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_NobleIdealist.label: duty-sentence
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_NobleIdealist.rule: This pawn tends to write one formal sentence around duty, obligation, or what was owed. Keep it restrained. For example: "It was unpleasant work, but it was mine to finish."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BitterLoner.label: sour-afterthought
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BitterLoner.rule: This pawn tends to write the event plainly, then add a short sour afterthought. For example: "Help came late. I noticed."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EagerSocialite.label: room-read
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EagerSocialite.rule: This pawn tends to write who noticed, who looked away, and how the room shifted. No invented dialogue. For example: "Mara saw it first, and the room went careful."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LedgerKeeper.label: plus-minus
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LedgerKeeper.rule: This pawn tends to write in gain/loss terms, sometimes with plus and minus words but not literal tables. For example: "Gained a closed door. Lost one good sleeve. Fair trade."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HardboiledGumshoe.label: weather-shadow
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HardboiledGumshoe.rule: This pawn tends to open with weather, light, or shadow in a clipped sentence, then state the event. For example: "Cold morning. Dark hall. The south door finally gave."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DoomsayingProphet.label: single-sign
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DoomsayingProphet.rule: This pawn tends to mark one real detail as a sign, then stop before predicting anything. For example: "The birds left early. I wrote that down."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ClinicalExperimenter.label: body-measure
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ClinicalExperimenter.rule: This pawn tends to record body signals as measurements before naming feeling. For example: "Hands steady. Breath short. Fear present but manageable."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_TheatricalTragedian.label: one-flourish
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_TheatricalTragedian.rule: This pawn tends to add one dramatic phrase to an otherwise clear account. Only one flourish. For example: "A miserable little victory, but a victory all the same."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SwaggeringBraggart.label: credit-at-end
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SwaggeringBraggart.rule: This pawn tends to state the fact, then claim a little credit in the final words without changing what happened. For example: "The door held. I helped, naturally."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_RamblingOldTimer.label: old-days-contrast
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_RamblingOldTimer.rule: This pawn tends to compare the event to the old days in one sentence, then return to today. For example: "Old camps patched leaks faster; this place held anyway."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SuperstitiousRitualist.rule: This pawn tends to count a small sign or habit before the event, often using a number. For example: "Three knocks before breakfast. Then the wall split."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DetachedPhilosopher.label: perhaps-question
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DetachedPhilosopher.rule: This pawn tends to turn the event into one quiet question with perhaps or one wonders. For example: "Perhaps repairs are how a place decides to stay."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ImagistMinimalist.label: three-fragments
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ImagistMinimalist.rule: This pawn tends to write three fragments built from concrete nouns, no explanation. For example: "Cold rail. Wet rope. Slow hands."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FracturedPatternSeer.label: repeat-pattern
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FracturedPatternSeer.rule: This pawn tends to repeat one sound, number, or word as a pattern while keeping the event visible. For example: "Three knocks. Three breaths. Door fixed."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WordSaladOracle.label: surreal-anchor
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WordSaladOracle.rule: This pawn tends to pair one strange image with one plain event fact. The fact must stay readable. For example: "The hour grew teeth; I fixed the latch."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_PlainspokenPoet.label: plain-last-image
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_PlainspokenPoet.rule: This pawn tends to write plain sentences and end on one ordinary image. For example: "We got through it. The fire was small but enough."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LowKeyRapper.label: soft-rhyme
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LowKeyRapper.rule: This pawn tends to use one small internal rhyme or beat, but no performance. For example: "Long shift, tight grip. Kept my head."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_VoidListener.label: silence-line
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_VoidListener.rule: This pawn tends to give silence its own short sentence. Keep the rest sparse. For example: "No one answered. The room did."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HollowWitness.label: hands-watching
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HollowWitness.rule: This pawn tends to describe their own body doing the event as if watched from outside. For example: "My hands finished it before I caught up."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BlackSignal.label: broken-repeat
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BlackSignal.rule: This pawn tends to write broken fragments with one repeated word. The event must remain clear. For example: "Still here. Wall held. Hold. Hold."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_UnsetClock.label: already-not-yet
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_UnsetClock.rule: This pawn tends to use already, again, or not yet to make time feel slightly folded while the event stays clear. For example: "It felt already done before I heard the crack."
